units.verbatim_text: Back then as a young man inevitably you would get into trouble sooner or later and you knew that as soon as you got caught for doing something wrong that they are going to beat you and it was it was an interesting thing because then they would bring you out in front of the class and and and God save you if it is like something that is so grave such a a massive crime and an injustice they bring you out in front of the entire school during the morning assembly and you know they would they would you know tell everybody what you did wrong and then they would say okay you know you now you're going to get whatever 12 strokes or 20 strokes or however many it was.
units.context: [Banky reflects on the harsh disciplinary practices in schools and their impact on young boys' understanding of masculinity.]

units.verbatim_text: There was only one rule for guys and guys especially you dare not cry you dare not shed a tear you dare not even flinch in fact you you had to do your absolute best no matter how much your bomb bomb was pinning you or your or your hand was painting you as they are beating you you had to hold back the tears you had to hold back any show of emotion or remorse because that's what to us that's what we we learned that being a man was all about being a man was about not showing emotions not showing feelings was about being strong and not you know not showing any kind of remorse for what you've done wrong or or regret or or tears or any kind of flinching whatsoever.
units.context: [Banky discusses the societal expectation for men to suppress emotions as a sign of strength.]

units.verbatim_text: We see that a lot of behaviors and problems that we see in men today can be traced back to the seeds that were planted from childhood because as young boys we learned that no matter how bad it hurts no matter what you did wrong or how bad the punishment hurts it is not okay to show or feel emotions and then we wonder why there are so many men today who use alcohol and drugs and all kinds of things to mask those same emotions as adults.
units.context: [Banky connects childhood emotional suppression to adult substance abuse among men.]

units.verbatim_text: We learned certain things at a young age that were so disastrous to the men that God created us to be and some men were never delivered from that some men never broke out of that some men never never let that go and they just it just got worse with time what about with our parents many of our parents especially Nigerian and African parents basically spent years forcing and coercing their children into becoming three things I know you know what those are what are the three things that most Nigerian parents and African parents were legendary for telling their children to become you have to become a doctor you have to become a lawyer or you have to become an engineer.
units.context: [Banky critiques the rigid career expectations imposed by Nigerian and African parents on their children.]

units.verbatim_text: Your job is to help your children discover their own dreams but so many parents air in in wanting their children to end up okay you end up forcing them into careers and into a a life that they never wanted for themselves and perhaps that God himself never even wanted for them and then we wonder why as adults there's so many men and women walking around who are depressed and who are resentful and who are sad and who are you know battling depression and all kinds of things because they are stuck living lives that they never planned for themselves or that God never planned for them.
units.context: [Banky argues that parental pressure can lead to adult depression and unfulfilled lives.]

units.verbatim_text: If any man is going to make the most out of himself he needs to come to terms with two things one your mission what is your mission in life and by asking that we're really asking what is your purpose in this world of 8 billion or so people what is it that you were created to do specifically what is your mission what is the reason for your existence existence has no meaning without purpose Dr. Miles Monroe defined purpose as the original intent in the mind of a creator that motivated him to create a particular item so your purpose or your mission is the why that explains the meaning for your existence.
units.context: [Banky emphasizes the importance of understanding one's mission and purpose in life.]

units.verbatim_text: Your purpose comes before the production okay your mission comes before the movement so you begin with the end result in mind that's the way that God works okay when you want to understand the purpose of equation just like we talked about this camera you really you really have to go to what the manufacturer had in mind from the beginning so if you want to understand your mission as a man you go to your maker the manufacturer has the end result in mind before the design in fact is the end result that inspires the design.
units.context: [Banky uses the analogy of a camera to explain how purpose precedes creation.]

units.verbatim_text: The ability to accomplish what God has has in mind for your life is already inbuilt into who you are it's already inside of you Moses was already called a deliverer by name and he already had deliverance skills inbuilt into him even before he knew what his mission was as the deliverer of Israel even before he knew how God would want him to go about it let's go to same let's say in exodus chapter 2 but let's go from verse 11 to verse 19.
units.context: [Banky asserts that individuals have innate abilities aligned with their God-given purpose.]
